Soil organic matter — Organic carbon content in the topsoil layer, up to 30cm depth, contained in (decomposed) plant and animal residues, tissues and cells (Nachtergaele et al., 2012). 'Soil organic matter' is an indicator of soil fertility and, thus, ecosystem productivity. Regions with low soil organic carbon need CSA practices that alleviate nutrient depletion such as measures to stop erosion to build up soil carbon through organic fertilizers and integrated practices such as agroforestry (Lal et al., 2011).

FAM_family_registry.name: Alley cropping
FAM_family_registry.definition: Planted rows of woody perennials with annual crops cultivated in the alleys between them.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Arable biophysical envelope + machinery/management access on cultivable land.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Planted silvoarable systems on cultivable land share a slope/soil/rainfall envelope and a management-access constraint; grouped by that shared limiting factor, not by tree species.

FAM_family_registry.name: Tree intercropping
FAM_family_registry.definition: Planted trees dispersed within or among annual crops on cultivable land.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Arable biophysical envelope + management access on cultivable land.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Same planted-on-cropland envelope as alley cropping; same dominant limiting factor.

FAM_family_registry.name: Planted boundary / live fence on cropland
FAM_family_registry.definition: Deliberately planted tree/shrub lines on the boundaries of cultivated fields.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Arable biophysical envelope + management access; established by planting.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Planted establishment on cropland places it in the silvoarable envelope family (distinct from F4, where the limiting factor is linear placement geometry rather than the arable envelope).

FAM_family_registry.name: Farmer-managed natural regeneration (FMNR)
FAM_family_registry.definition: Re-establishing tree cover by protecting and managing natural regrowth from living stumps, roots and seed, rather than planting.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Regeneration potential — remnant rootstock, seedbank, seed-source proximity, and tenure security to protect regrowth.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Limited by regeneration potential, not the planting envelope; a different variable set (woody-cover baseline/change, seed-source distance, tenure), so it cannot be an override of F1.

FAM_family_registry.name: Parkland tree retention
FAM_family_registry.definition: Retaining/regenerating scattered mature trees on cropland (e.g. Faidherbia parkland) by protecting natural regeneration.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Regeneration potential + tenure (retention/ANR), not deliberate planting.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Retained parkland is regeneration-driven (kin to ANR/FMNR); a planted parkland would instead sit in F1. Keyed by establishment driver, not appearance.

FAM_family_registry.name: Assisted natural regeneration on farmland
FAM_family_registry.definition: Active assistance (weeding, protection, thinning) to accelerate natural regeneration on farmland.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Regeneration potential — seedbank/rootstock, seed source, tenure.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Same regeneration-potential limiting factor as FMNR and parkland retention.

FAM_family_registry.name: Planted silvopasture
FAM_family_registry.definition: Deliberate integration of planted trees with pasture and grazing livestock.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Biophysical envelope PLUS grazing context and forage/livestock variables.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Needs additional variables (forage, grazing pressure, livestock) beyond the silvoarable envelope; overrides only re-parameterise existing variables, so it is its own family.

FAM_family_registry.name: Agrosilvopasture
FAM_family_registry.definition: Combined trees + crops + livestock on the same land unit.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Shares the grazing-context limiting factor with planted silvopasture.

FAM_family_registry.name: Windbreaks & shelterbelts
FAM_family_registry.definition: Tree/shrub lines positioned to reduce wind exposure across fields.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Linear-feature geometry — wind exposure and field-boundary placement.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Placement (line within a candidate zone) is the limiting logic, not arable envelope; footprint reported as candidate length/density, never pixel area.

FAM_family_registry.name: Contour & vegetative buffers
FAM_family_registry.definition: Tree/shrub lines along contours or slope-flow lines to reduce erosion and runoff.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Linear-feature geometry — slope/flow lines + boundary placement.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Same linear-placement limiting factor as windbreaks; candidate length/density footprint.

FAM_family_registry.name: Shaded perennial-crop system
FAM_family_registry.definition: Shade trees grown over an existing perennial cash crop. The understorey crop (coffee, cocoa, tea, cardamom, pepper, ...) is a PARAMETER of the family, scoped per AOI - not a separate subpractice.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: The host crop's observed distribution / production (where the crop is actually grown), plus shade-as-adaptation at the climate-stressed edge.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: One family, crop as a parameter (run per candidate crop, combine by max retaining the driving crop) - the way F1 is not split by tree species. Gated by the crop's observed distribution (MapSPAM / GAEZ / EO commodity maps / ag-stats), not a modelled niche; bioclimatic-envelope modelling is the fallback only where distribution data are absent. Crops are not blended into one envelope (coffee highlands and cocoa lowlands are nearly disjoint).

FAM_family_registry.name: Multistrata homegardens
FAM_family_registry.definition: Intensive multistrata tree-crop-livestock gardens adjacent to dwellings.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Settlement and water proximity at sub-pixel scale.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Sub-pixel and proximity-driven; a raster suitability surface misleads, so handled qualitatively via the scorecard + M6 note rather than mapped.

FAM_family_registry.name: Macro-catchment water harvesting
FAM_family_registry.definition: Structures that collect runoff from a catchment larger than the cultivated/storage area (e.g. check dams, farm ponds).
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Runoff generation + storable terrain (slope, drainage, soil permeability).
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Runoff-catchment WH practices share a runoff-generation + storage-terrain limiting factor; reported as an applicability zone, not pixel area.

T1_data_registry.citation: Fick, S.E. & Hijmans, R.J. (2017). WorldClim 2: new 1km spatial resolution climate surfaces for global land areas. International Journal of Climatology, 37(12), 4302–4315.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Resample to 1 km COG, clip to AOI. BIO1=Annual Mean Temp; BIO12=Annual Precipitation; BIO15=Precipitation Seasonality most relevant for agroforestry suitability.
T1_data_registry.limitations: 30-year climatological mean; does not capture interannual variability or extremes. Resolution may mask local topographic effects. Not for: Site-level microclimate analysis or real-time weather monitoring
T1_data_registry.description: Global gridded bioclimatic variables (BIO1-BIO19) derived from 1970-2000 monthly temperature and precipitation.

T1_data_registry.citation: Karger, D.N. et al. (2017). Climatologies at high resolution for the earth's land surface areas (CHELSA). Scientific Data, 4, 170122.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Resample to 1 km COG. CHELSA-BIOCLIM+ Version 1.2 used by Yadav et al. (2024) for agroforestry suitability in Punjab. Use Bio1 (Annual Mean Temp), Bio12 (Annual Precipitation), Tmax_Jan, Tmax_May.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Statistical downscaling from ERA5; may show artefacts in complex terrain. Less validated over Africa and Central Asia. Not for: Short-term weather forecasting or sub-field precision agriculture
T1_data_registry.description: Bias-corrected high-resolution global bioclimatic variables, climatology 1981-2010 (~1 km).

T1_data_registry.citation: Karger, D.N. et al. (2023). CHELSA-W5E5: Daily 1 km meteorological forcing data for climate impact studies. Earth System Science Data, 15, 2445–2464.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Use delta-change approach relative to baseline. Clip to project AOI. Ensemble mean of available CMIP6 GCMs preferred.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Ensemble spread is wide in tropical regions. Precipitation projections carry higher uncertainty than temperature. Not for: Single-GCM deterministic planning; local infrastructure design
T1_data_registry.description: CHELSA bioclimatic variables under SSP2-4.5 around 2050 (CMIP6 ensemble downscaled).

T1_data_registry.citation: Vicente-Serrano, S.M. et al. (2010). A Multiscalar Drought Index Sensitive to Global Warming: The Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index. Journal of Climate, 23(7), 1696–1718.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Extract SPEI-12 (12-month accumulation window) for drought risk. Compute mean and trend over 1981–2010 baseline. Bilinearly resample to 1 km for overlay.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Very coarse native resolution (0.5°). Misses sub-regional drought heterogeneity. PET estimation method affects values. Not for: Farm-level drought monitoring; flash drought detection
T1_data_registry.description: Standardized Precipitation-Evapotranspiration Index, global ~55 km, monthly 1901-present.

T1_data_registry.citation: Poggio, L. et al. (2021). SoilGrids 2.0: producing soil information for the globe with quantified spatial uncertainty. SOIL, 7, 217–240.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Extract layers at 0–30 cm depth (topsoil) and 30–60 cm (subsoil). Resample to 1 km. pH, SOC, clay content, CEC are primary agroforestry suitability constraints per Ahmad et al. (2020), Chuma et al. (2021), Haile (2024).
T1_data_registry.limitations: Prediction uncertainty is high in data-sparse regions (much of sub-Saharan Africa). Point observations biased toward accessible areas. Temporal static — does not capture land-use change effects on soil. Not for: Precision soil management at plot level; nutrient management plans without ground-truthing
T1_data_registry.description: Global digital soil mapping (250 m) of physical and chemical soil properties at six depths.

T1_data_registry.citation: FAO/IIASA/ISRIC/ISSCAS/JRC (2023). Harmonized World Soil Database version 2.0. FAO, Rome, Italy & IIASA, Laxenburg, Austria.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Used by Ahmad et al. (2020) as primary soil input for South Asia agroforestry suitability. Join soil mapping unit attributes on raster values. Extract topsoil properties for suitability scoring.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Coarser conceptual resolution than SoilGrids despite same pixel size — many large mapping units contain internally variable soils. Outdated in rapidly changing agricultural areas. Not for: Detailed site characterisation; soil carbon accounting
T1_data_registry.description: Harmonized World Soil Database v2 — global ~1 km soil units with property attributes.

T1_data_registry.citation: Farr, T.G. et al. (2007). The Shuttle Radar Topography Mission. Reviews of Geophysics, 45(2), RG2004.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Derive slope (degrees) and aspect from DEM in GEE. Slope is primary biophysical constraint for agroforestry machinery access and erosion risk. Used across nearly all 23 AF papers in stocktake (Ahmad et al. 2017, 2018, 2020; Chuma et al. 2021; Haile 2024; Nath et al. 2021; Mushtaq et al. 2023; Seja et al. 2022; Wotlolan et al. 2021).
T1_data_registry.limitations: Voids in high-relief areas filled with ASTER or other interpolation methods — check for artefacts. Vegetation canopy not filtered — potential overestimation of elevation under forest cover. Not for: Hydraulic modelling requiring bare-earth DEM; landslide hazard at plot scale
T1_data_registry.description: Shuttle Radar Topography Mission digital elevation model, 3 arc-second (~90 m), 56°S-60°N.

T1_data_registry.citation: Zanaga, D. et al. (2022). ESA WorldCover 10m 2021 v200. Zenodo.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Reclassify to agricultural mask (cropland, grassland, sparse vegetation) to define eligible agroforestry conversion area. Exclude built-up, water bodies, bare soil with high salinity. Aggregate to 100m or 1km for pipeline. Used as LULC base by multiple AF studies.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Mixed-pixel confusion in fragmented agricultural landscapes. Annual snapshot — does not capture seasonal cultivation. Accuracy lower in arid landscapes and under cloud cover. Not for: Crop-type mapping; sub-field land use classification
T1_data_registry.description: ESA WorldCover 10 m global land-cover map (2021), Sentinel-1/2 derived 11-class classification.

T1_data_registry.dataset_name: Global Aridity Index and PET v3 (CGIAR-CSI)
T1_data_registry.citation: Trabucco, A. & Zomer, R. (2019). Global Aridity Index and Potential Evapotranspiration (ET0) Climate Database v2. CGIAR-CSI GeoPortal.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: AI < 0.5 = dryland; used to mask unsuitable extreme arid zones for agroforestry. Ahmad et al. (2020) use aridity index as key suitability layer for South Asia. Nath et al. (2021) use Global Aridity Index for Himalayan region.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Derived from WorldClim 1970–2000 climatology — does not reflect recent drying trends. PET estimated with Penman-Monteith assuming reference crop. Not for: Real-time drought monitoring; current water stress assessment
T1_data_registry.description: Global-Aridity Index v3 — long-term P/PET ratio at ~1 km (CGIAR-CSI).

T1_data_registry.citation: UNEP-WCMC & IUCN (2024). Protected Planet: The World Database on Protected Areas (WDPA). Cambridge: UNEP-WCMC and IUCN.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Rasterize to 100m. Hard exclusion mask for IUCN categories Ia, Ib, II for agroforestry suitability (conversion not permitted). Categories V, VI may allow agroforestry within management plans — flag as conditional. Used by Chuma et al. (2021) as exclusion layer around Itombwe Nature Reserve.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Boundary data quality varies by country. Some protected areas are paper parks with little enforcement. Does not capture community conserved areas (OECMs) comprehensively. Not for: Legal compliance decisions; site-level permitting
T1_data_registry.description: World Database on Protected Areas — global polygons of legally protected sites (UNEP-WCMC / IUCN).

T1_data_registry.citation: OpenStreetMap contributors (2024). OpenStreetMap. www.openstreetmap.org.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Compute Euclidean distance to nearest road of any class. Used by Chuma et al. (2021) as infrastructure accessibility layer. Apply 5km threshold as moderate accessibility constraint for agroforestry market access. is_scenario_candidate = true (road investment can change this).
T1_data_registry.limitations: OSM coverage quality is highly variable in rural sub-Saharan Africa, Central Asia, and remote tropical regions — significant under-mapping in key project areas. Validate against national road datasets where possible. Not for: Routing or logistics planning; travel-time modelling without validation
T1_data_registry.description: OpenStreetMap global road network vector lines, multiple admin levels.

T1_data_registry.citation: WorldPop (2020). Global High Resolution Population Denominators Project - Funded by The Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation (OPP1134076). University of Southampton.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Aggregate to 1 km for pipeline. Use as proxy for population exposure in climate risk and as denominator for opportunity space poverty metrics.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Constrained model relies on settlement/building footprint mapping — may undercount nomadic/dispersed rural populations. Accuracy varies with census quality of input data. Not for: Individual household targeting; electoral boundary analysis
T1_data_registry.description: Global gridded population estimates at 100 m for the year stated (WorldPop).

T1_data_registry.dataset_name: FAO Global Soil Organic Carbon Map (GSOCmap) v1.6.1
T1_data_registry.citation: FAO & ITPS (2018). Global Soil Organic Carbon Map (GSOCmap) v1.6.1. Technical Report. FAO, Rome.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Use as carbon co-benefit layer in opportunity space. Mendonça et al. (2023) use SOC sequestration potential as an agroforestry prioritisation criterion. Kiziridis et al. (2026) map silvopastoral carbon sequestration. Complement with FAO GSOCseq for sequestration rate.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Country submissions vary in quality and methodology. National and sub-national SOC maps (where available) should be used in preference. Not for: Carbon crediting baselines without country-specific validation; REDD+ baseline setting
T1_data_registry.description: FAO Global Soil Organic Carbon map — country-validated 1 km topsoil (0-30 cm) SOC stocks.

T1_data_registry.dataset_name: FAO-IIASA Global Agro-Ecological Zones v4 (33-class AEZ)
T1_data_registry.citation: FAO & IIASA (2021). Global Agro-Ecological Zones – GAEZ v4. FAO, Rome & IIASA, Laxenburg.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Use for AEZ context classification in T7. Key AEZ groups for agroforestry context overrides: humid tropics, sub-humid tropics, semi-arid, highland tropics.
T1_data_registry.limitations: AEZ boundaries are climate-envelope based and do not capture soil or management diversity within zones. Not for: Crop yield modelling at field scale
T1_data_registry.description: Global Agro-Ecological Zones v4 — 33-class agro-ecological zoning at ~1 km.

T1_data_registry.dataset_name: FAO/IFAD Farming Systems of the World (Dixon et al. 2001 / updated)
T1_data_registry.citation: Dixon, J., Gulliver, A. & Gibbon, D. (2001). Farming Systems and Poverty: Improving Farmers' Livelihoods in a Changing World. FAO and World Bank, Rome & Washington D.C.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Rasterize at 10 km then resample to 1 km with majority resampling. Used to populate T7 farming system contexts and to apply context-specific suitability overrides in T4.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Classification is 20+ years old; agricultural systems have shifted significantly in some regions. Mixed-farming boundaries are expert-delineated and coarse. Not for: Subnational farming system analysis without local validation
T1_data_registry.description: FAO Dixon farming-systems typology for developing regions (Africa/Asia/Latin America).

T1_data_registry.dataset_name: World Bank Poverty and Inequality Platform — Subnational Poverty Estimates
T1_data_registry.citation: World Bank (2024). Poverty and Inequality Platform (PIP). World Bank Group, Washington DC.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Interpolate to raster using admin boundary weights and WorldPop population density. Combine with spatial poverty maps (e.g. CGIAR CELL5M poverty layers) for higher-resolution estimates.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Sub-national resolution limited to admin-1 or -2 in most countries. Survey years vary. Does not capture within-admin spatial variation. Not for: Household targeting; beneficiary selection for project implementation
T1_data_registry.description: World Bank PIP subnational poverty headcount estimates at admin-1 (admin-2 where available).

T1_data_registry.citation: Hansen, M.C. et al. (2013). High-Resolution Global Maps of 21st-Century Forest Cover Change. Science, 342(6160), 850–853.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Current tree cover = baseline (2000) − loss + gain. Used to identify areas below 10% tree cover as agroforestry expansion opportunity and areas with existing tree cover for context. Singh et al. (2024) and Ahmad et al. (2020) use Hansen tree cover as AF suitability input.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Confusion between natural forest and plantation/agroforestry systems. Understory trees in agroforestry are often missed at 30m resolution. Not for: Legal deforestation monitoring for jurisdictional REDD+; plot-level timber volume
T1_data_registry.description: Global Forest Change tree cover and loss layers (Hansen et al.) at 30 m.

T1_data_registry.citation: Fick, S.E. & Hijmans, R.J. (2017). WorldClim 2: new 1km spatial resolution climate surfaces for global land areas. International Journal of Climatology, 37(12), 4302-4315.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Rainfall is the second most frequent input variable (38 of 85 papers). Extract mean annual precipitation and wet-season monthly totals. Use for SCS-CN runoff depth calculation combined with HSG soil map. Buraihi & Shariff (2015) use projected precipitation to assess future RWH viability under climate change.
T1_data_registry.limitations: 30-year climatological mean; interannual variability not captured. Resolution too coarse for localised orographic precipitation in complex terrain. Not for: Flood frequency analysis; design-storm estimation for dam sizing
T1_data_registry.description: Climate Hazards Group InfraRed Precipitation with Stations, ~5 km daily precipitation 1981-present.

T1_data_registry.dataset_name: HydroSHEDS Watershed Boundaries and River Network
T1_data_registry.citation: Lehner, B. et al. (2008). New global hydrography derived from spaceborne elevation data. Eos, Transactions AGU, 89(10), 93-94.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Use HydroBASINS level 8-10 for sub-watershed delineation context. Stream order used as a primary RWH site-type discriminator: first/second-order streams suit check dams and gully plugs; third/fourth-order streams suit percolation tanks; larger streams suit small dams. Kumar et al. (2018) and Singh et al. (2016) use stream order as a primary MCDA criterion.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Derived from 3-arc-second SRTM; misses ephemeral channels in flat arid terrain. Resolution insufficient for first-order gully mapping. Not for: Detailed gully morphometry; flash flood routing
T1_data_registry.description: Composite SCS curve-number runoff potential surface combining land-cover and hydrologic soil group.

T1_data_registry.citation: Farr et al. (2007). SRTM. Reviews of Geophysics.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: WH module alias for SRTM. See srtm_dem_90m entry for full notes.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Voids in high-relief terrain. Not for: Engineering design
T1_data_registry.description: Shuttle Radar Topography Mission digital elevation model, 1 arc-second (~30 m), 56°S-60°N.

T1_data_registry.citation: Fick & Hijmans (2017). WorldClim 2. International Journal of Climatology.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Extract mean annual precipitation and wet-season monthly totals for SCS-CN runoff calculation.
T1_data_registry.limitations: 30-year climatological mean; interannual variability not captured. Not for: Flood frequency analysis; design-storm estimation
T1_data_registry.description: WorldClim v2 monthly precipitation (1970-2000) at ~1 km.

T1_data_registry.citation: IFPRI (2024) MapSPAM 2020 Global Spatially-Disaggregated Crop Production.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Use harvested-area fraction as graded crop-presence constraint.
T1_data_registry.limitations: ~10 km; allocation-model estimate, not observed. Coarse for sub-national targeting. Not for: plot siting.
T1_data_registry.description: MapSPAM 2020 — gridded crop production allocation for cocoa at ~10 km.

T1_data_registry.citation: GADM (2022) Database of Global Administrative Areas v4.1.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Country/region polygons for AOI + admin context scoping.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Boundary vintages vary by country; non-commercial licence. Not for: dispute adjudication.
T1_data_registry.description: GADM Database of Global Administrative Areas v4.1 — admin-0/1/2/3 boundary polygons.

T1_data_registry.citation: Potapov, P., Turubanova, S., Hansen, M. C., Tyukavina, A., Zalles, V., Khan, A., Song, X.-P., Pickens, A., Shen, Q., & Cortez, J. (2022). Global maps of cropland extent and change show accelerated cropland expansion in the twenty-first century. Nature Food, 3, 19-28.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Use as binary cropland presence layer.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Cropland definition includes fallow + temporary cropping; rotational use may misclassify.
T1_data_registry.description: Potapov et al. (2022) global cropland extent for the year 2019 at 30m, derived from Landsat time series. Binary cropland mask.

T1_data_registry.citation: Van Tricht, K., Degerickx, J., Gilliams, S., Zanaga, D., Battude, M., Grosu, A., Brombacher, J., Pelayo, Y. M., Lesiv, M., Buchhorn, M., Smets, B., Tarko, A., Szantoi, Z., Bauwens, I., Defourny, P., & Szilagyi, I. (2023). WorldCereal: a dynamic open-source system for global-scale, seasonal, and reproducible crop and irrigation mapping. Earth System Science Data, 15, 5491-5515.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Multi-layer: active_cropland, temporary_crops, irrigation. Pick layer per use.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Calibration variable by region; irrigation class is presence not intensity.
T1_data_registry.description: ESA WorldCereal product (2020-2021 season) at 10m: active cropland + 4 seasonal crop classes (temporary crops, irrigated/rainfed). Used here as cropland + irrigation auxiliary.

T1_data_registry.dataset_name: Gridded Livestock of the World v4 (GLW4, 2020)
T1_data_registry.citation: Gilbert, M., Nicolas, G., Cinardi, G., Van Boeckel, T. P., Vanwambeke, S. O., Wint, G. R. W., & Robinson, T. P. (2018). Global distribution data for cattle, buffaloes, horses, sheep, goats, pigs, chickens and ducks in 2010. Scientific Data, 5, 180227. (v4 update 2024).
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Sum across ruminant species (cattle + buffalo + sheep + goats) for total ruminant density; convert TLU using ILRI weights for fair comparison.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Modelled (dasymetric), not observed; uncertainty highest in pastoral / mobile-herd contexts.
T1_data_registry.description: FAO/ILRI/UMR-SELMET gridded livestock density (per species: cattle, buffalo, sheep, goats, pigs, chickens, ducks, horses) at ~10 km, year 2015 baseline (v4 release 2024).

T1_data_registry.dataset_name: FAO Global Map of Irrigation Areas v5 (GMIA)
T1_data_registry.citation: Siebert, S., Henrich, V., Frenken, K., & Burke, J. (2013). Update of the digital global map of irrigation areas to version 5. FAO/Rheinische Friedrich-Wilhelms-Universität Bonn.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Area equipped for irrigation is potential, not actual irrigation use; combine with WorldCereal irrigation for refinement.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Coarse resolution; lags real-world updates; rainfed mosaics may be misclassified at coarse aggregation.
T1_data_registry.description: FAO/Rheinische Friedrich-Wilhelms-Universität Bonn GMIA v5 — area equipped for irrigation as % of cell, at 5 arc-minute (~10 km).

T1_data_registry.citation: Newbold, T., Hudson, L. N., Arnell, A. P., Contu, S., De Palma, A., Ferrier, S., ... & Purvis, A. (2016). Has land use pushed terrestrial biodiversity beyond the planetary boundary? A global assessment. Science, 353(6296), 288-291.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: BII is a 0-100 (or 0-1) intactness index — invert if framing biodiversity_priority as 'higher = more concern' (i.e. priority = 1 - intactness, so lowest-intactness areas surface).
T1_data_registry.limitations: Modelled (PREDICTS database); systematic uncertainty in under-sampled biomes. Doesn't capture rare-species hotspots — pair with KBA polygons as a binary high-priority flag.
T1_data_registry.description: Global Biodiversity Intactness Index — modelled remaining proportion of local biodiversity intactness vs an undisturbed baseline, ~1 km global gridded surface.

T1_data_registry.citation: Didan, K. (2021). MODIS/Terra Vegetation Indices 16-Day L3 Global 250m SIN Grid V061. NASA EOSDIS Land Processes DAAC.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Aggregate to annual mean / max-NDVI / range as needed. Mask cloud-flagged pixels per QA layer.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Coarser than Sentinel-2; for finer NDVI use Sentinel-2 SR. Cloud contamination in tropics.
T1_data_registry.description: NASA MODIS Terra Vegetation Indices MOD13Q1 — 16-day composite NDVI at 250m. The de facto standard NDVI surface for time-series + annual aggregation.

T1_data_registry.citation: Fick, S. E., & Hijmans, R. J. (2017). WorldClim 2: new 1-km spatial resolution climate surfaces for global land areas. International Journal of Climatology, 37(12), 4302-4315.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Sum across months or take annual mean as appropriate.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Modelled (not measured) at any specific location; baseline only, no climate projections in this release.
T1_data_registry.description: WorldClim v2.1 monthly solar radiation surface, ~1 km, 1970-2000 climatology. kJ/m²/day.

T1_data_registry.citation: Pesaresi, M., & Politis, P. (2023). GHS-BUILT-S R2023A — GHS built-up surface grid, derived from Sentinel-2 composite and Landsat, multitemporal (1975-2030). European Commission, Joint Research Centre (JRC).
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Pick the epoch nearest to the run year; threshold built-up share if a binary mask is needed.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Smallholder rural settlement under-detected at 100m; complements WorldPop population for a built+population framing.
T1_data_registry.description: Joint Research Centre Global Human Settlement Layer (GHSL) built-up surface — global multi-temporal product at 100m, 5-year epochs from 1975 to present.

T1_data_registry.dataset_name: Global accessibility to cities — Weiss et al. (Nelson) 2018, 2015 baseline
T1_data_registry.citation: Weiss, D. J., et al. (2018). A global map of travel time to cities to assess inequalities in accessibility in 2015. Nature, 553, 333-336.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Travel time minutes; invert if framing as accessibility (1 / time) for the suitability surface.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Road network from OSM ~2016; coverage variable in remote regions; not updated for post-2016 infrastructure.
T1_data_registry.description: Global travel time to the nearest city of 50,000+ inhabitants at ~1 km. Built from road network, terrain, surface friction and city polygons.

T1_data_registry.citation: Elvidge, C. D., et al. (2021). Annual time series of global VIIRS nighttime lights derived from monthly averages: 2012 to 2019. Remote Sensing, 13(5), 922.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Use as continuous radiance or threshold (e.g. >1 nW/cm²/sr) for binary 'grid-served'.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Proxy only — captures lit pavement + industrial flaring, not domestic-only electrification.
T1_data_registry.description: NOAA Earth Observation Group VIIRS DNB annual cloud-free composite at ~500m — radiance values used as electrification proxy.

T1_data_registry.dataset_name: LandMark — Global Platform of Indigenous & Community Lands
T1_data_registry.citation: LandMark Consortium (2024). LandMark: Global Platform of Indigenous and Community Lands. World Resources Institute and partners.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Spatial join with AOI; flag overlap as FPIC_REQUIRED for M6 hand-off.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Coverage is patchy — only countries where IPLC mapping is documented; absence ≠ no IPLC presence.
T1_data_registry.description: Global vector platform of indigenous and community lands — formal recognition status, customary tenure boundaries.

T1_data_registry.dataset_name: ACLED — Armed Conflict Location & Event Data
T1_data_registry.citation: Raleigh, C., Linke, A., Hegre, H., & Karlsen, J. (2010). Introducing ACLED-Armed Conflict Location and Event Data. Journal of Peace Research, 47(5), 651-660.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Aggregate events per km² over a rolling window (e.g. 5 years); pair with WB FCV admin0 list.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Event detection bias (media-coverage-dependent); under-reporting in less-mediated conflicts.
T1_data_registry.description: Point-event conflict dataset (battles, violence, riots, protests) with daily updates. Used here as gridded event density for fragility framing.

T1_data_registry.citation: Kaufmann, D., Kraay, A., & Mastruzzi, M. (2010). The Worldwide Governance Indicators: Methodology and Analytical Issues. World Bank Policy Research Working Paper 5430.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Use the 'government effectiveness' indicator as the default extension/governance proxy; composite across all six if a broader index is needed.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Country-level only; no subnational variation — qualified-flag-only for in-country MCDA differentiation (per T5 spatial-grain rule).
T1_data_registry.description: WB WGI — six aggregate governance indicators (voice/accountability, political stability, government effectiveness, regulatory quality, rule of law, control of corruption), admin0, annual.

T1_data_registry.citation: Demirgüç-Kunt, A., Klapper, L., Singer, D., & Ansar, S. (2022). The Global Findex Database 2021: Financial Inclusion, Digital Payments, and Resilience in the Age of COVID-19. World Bank.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Use 'account ownership' or 'borrowed from formal financial institution' as the rural-finance proxy; subnational requires_upload via national surveys.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Survey-based, country-level only; subnational requires_upload.
T1_data_registry.description: WB Global Findex — financial inclusion indicators (account ownership, savings, credit, mobile money). Survey-based, admin0, triennial.

T1_data_registry.citation: FAO (2024). FAOSTAT statistical database. Rome: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Use producer prices + trade volumes as a market-depth proxy; subnational requires country-team uploads.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Admin0 granularity; subnational variation invisible.
T1_data_registry.description: FAO statistical database — country agricultural production, trade, prices, food balances. Admin0, annual.

T1_data_registry.citation: ILO (2024). ILOSTAT — Labour Statistics. International Labour Organization, Geneva.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Use 'agricultural employment as % of total employment' as the rural-labour-availability framing; pair with LSMS-ISA for subnational where present.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Country-level only; subnational variation requires LSMS-ISA or country uploads.
T1_data_registry.description: International Labour Organization ILOSTAT — labour-force participation, agricultural employment share, wage indicators. Admin0, annual.

T1_data_registry.dataset_name: JRC Global Flood Hazard Maps (Dottori et al. 2016)
T1_data_registry.citation: Dottori, F., Salamon, P., Bianchi, A., Alfieri, L., Hirpa, F. A., & Feyen, L. (2016). Development and evaluation of a framework for global flood hazard mapping. Advances in Water Resources, 94, 87-102.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Pick return-period band (100y default for scoping); model assumes existing flood protection per FLOPROS.
T1_data_registry.limitations: River flooding only — pluvial/urban + coastal flooding need separate layers (e.g. WRI Aqueduct Coastal). Hydrological-model uncertainty in data-sparse basins.
T1_data_registry.description: JRC global river-flood hazard maps — return-period flood depth grids (e.g. 100-year, 500-year) at ~30 arc-second (~1 km).

T1_data_registry.citation: Kuzma, S., Saccoccia, L., & Chertock, M. (2023). 25 Countries, Housing One-quarter of the Population, Face Extremely High Water Stress. World Resources Institute (Aqueduct 4.0).
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Use the baseline-water-stress (BWS) layer; SSP scenarios available for future projections.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Basin/admin1 granularity; not pixel-level.
T1_data_registry.description: World Resources Institute Aqueduct 4.0 baseline water-stress index — ratio of withdrawals to renewable supply, at HydroSHEDS basin level + admin1.

T1_data_registry.citation: UNDP (2024). Gender Inequality Index (GII). Human Development Report Office, United Nations Development Programme.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: National-flag layer; subnational disaggregation requires_upload (LSMS, DHS).
T1_data_registry.limitations: Admin0 only — no subnational variation. Per T5 spatial-grain rule, surfaces as qualified flag, not hotspot driver.
T1_data_registry.description: UNDP Human Development Report Office Gender Inequality Index — composite of reproductive health, empowerment, labour-market gender gap. Admin0, annual.

T1_data_registry.dataset_name: Lesiv et al. Global field-size / smallholder share
T1_data_registry.citation: Lesiv, M., Bayas, J. C. L., See, L., Duerauer, M., Dahlia, D., Durando, N., et al. (2019). Estimating the global distribution of field size using crowdsourcing. Global Change Biology, 25, 174-186.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Use field-size class or smallholder fraction depending on T5 framing.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Crowdsourced — sampling uneven; complements national agricultural censuses rather than replaces them.
T1_data_registry.description: Lesiv et al. (2019) global field-size and smallholder-share dataset — derived from crowdsourced photo-interpretation, ~1 km. Field-size class + smallholder fraction.

T1_data_registry.dataset_name: MapSPAM 2017 v2.0 — global crop production value
T1_data_registry.citation: International Food Policy Research Institute (IFPRI) (2024). Global Spatially-Disaggregated Crop Production Statistics Data for 2017 Version 2.0. Harvard Dataverse.
T1_data_registry.preprocessing_notes: Sum economic value across crops for a generic production-value layer; per-crop layers also available.
T1_data_registry.limitations: Modelled allocation (not surveyed); coarse ~10 km; specific tree-perennial coverage weak (use Forest Data Partnership for cocoa/coffee/palm).
T1_data_registry.description: IFPRI/IIASA Spatial Production Allocation Model (MapSPAM) v2.0 — gridded crop area, yield, production, value for 42 crops at ~10 km.

T2_climate_risk.justification: SPEI-12 is the standard metric for agricultural drought, capturing cumulative moisture deficit over a 12-month window. It is directly relevant to agroforestry systems where tree establishment and early canopy development are sensitive to prolonged dry periods. Brandt et al. (2015) and Mendonça et al. (2022) use precipitation/aridity exposure as the primary climate hazard layer in CSA/agroforestry suitability frameworks. Drought is the dominant climate barrier for agroforestry adoption in semi-arid Africa and South Asia (Ahmad et al. 2020).

Extreme heat events damage tree seedlings and reduce photosynthetic efficiency. Yadav et al. (2024) use Tmax_May (hottest pre-monsoon month) and Tmax_Jan as key inputs to AHP-based agroforestry suitability in Punjab. BIO5 (max temp of warmest month) is the most agriculturally relevant temperature extreme for heat stress assessment.

Heavy rainfall events cause soil waterlogging and root damage in agroforestry systems, particularly in low-lying and floodplain areas. Castle et al. (2025) treat flood hazard as a suitability constraint for agroforestry in the US Midwest, excluding floodplain areas. Baldwin et al. (2022) integrate flood inundation as a key exposure layer. BIO13 (wettest month precipitation) is a practical proxy for flood-generating rainfall intensity at 1 km resolution.

Agricultural land is the primary exposure element for climate risks affecting agroforestry investment. Higher proportions of cropland and pasture indicate greater exposure of rural livelihoods to climate hazards. Used implicitly or explicitly across the majority of the 23 agroforestry stocktake papers as the base land-use layer defining the eligible intervention space.

Higher rural population density amplifies the human exposure dimension of climate risk and also signals greater potential beneficiary reach of agroforestry investments. Applied as a shared variable — used in risk (exposure component) and retained in opportunity space (beneficiary reach) with double_count_risk guard set to 'shared'.

Soils with low organic carbon and high sand content are more sensitive to drought and erosion, and agroforestry systems on such soils are more vulnerable to climate shocks. Ahmad & Goparaju (2017) and Haile (2024) identify nutrient availability and soil quality as primary sensitivity indicators for agroforestry viability. Low-nutrient capital soils exhibit high sensitivity in the CrossBoundary fragile_gains archetype.

Access to roads and markets enables farmers to access inputs, extension services, and output markets — all of which increase adaptive capacity to climate shocks. Remoteness (high road distance) reduces adaptive capacity. Chuma et al. (2021) use distance to roads as an agroforestry suitability criterion. Castle et al. (2025) include infrastructure connectivity as a social-ecological suitability layer.

Future drought risk under SSP2-4.5 is the key forward-looking hazard for long-horizon agroforestry investments. Mendonça et al. (2022) incorporate future climate projections (precipitation anomaly 2050) as a driver of agroforestry prioritisation. Given agroforestry establishment periods of 5–15 years, decisions made today need to account for mid-century climate conditions.

High runoff depth is simultaneously a flood hazard indicator AND the primary measure of water harvesting opportunity. Terencio et al. (2017) weight overflood risk as a suitability barrier. Abd-el-Kader et al. (2023) and Ahmad et al. (2024) explicitly target high-runoff zones as RWH sites for flood-risk adaptation. Shared variable - used in risk (flood hazard) and opportunity space (RWH potential).

High inter-annual rainfall variability exposes rain-fed systems to erratic water availability, increasing the value of storage. Mahmoud et al. (2014) use rainfall variability in RWH site prioritisation. Khan et al. (2022) quantify climate-driven reductions in reliable rainfall as a key exposure driver.

High-permeability soils have low water retention, making systems more sensitive to drought. Vema et al. (2019) use soil permeability as a direct suitability input. Bera & Mukhopadhyay (2023) include soil texture as a key sensitivity variable.

T5_opportunity_space.ttl_priority_description: Long-term SPEI-based drought severity / frequency — areas where dry climatic stress is high.
Return-period flood depth/extent — areas exposed to riverine/pluvial flooding.
Frequency of extreme-heat days (Tmax or heat-index) — heat-stress exposure for crops, livestock and labour.
RUSLE-class soil-erosion risk — where soil-conservation NbS would have the largest mitigation effect.
FAO GSOCseq-style additional-SOC potential — where NbS would deliver the largest carbon co-benefit.

Biodiversity Intactness Index (BII, Newbold et al. 2016) at ~1 km global. Use (1 − BII) as priority: lower-intactness pixels surface as higher concern. KBA polygons recommended as supplementary high-priority binary flag.
Composite water-stress index — exposure to agricultural / human water-availability constraints.
WB PIP subnational poverty headcount — concentration of rural poor.
Shortfall vs attainable productivity for the dominant farming system. Per-system metric resolved via BIND (yield gap / NPP gap / mixed).
Share of livelihoods / employment dependent on the agricultural sector — where ag-NbS shocks/benefits hit hardest.
National-level gender-inequity index. Subnational disaggregation requires_upload.

Indigenous & community lands within the opportunity space (LandMark + WWF/ICCA). Subnational equity-priority signal — surfaces marginalised communities and triggers FPIC/ESS7 flag for M6 hand-off. Coverage patchy — absence ≠ no IPLC presence.
Rural population density — context for hand-off (how many people are in the hotspot).
Accessibility — travel time to large markets. Context, not a hotspot driver.
Mean farm size / smallholder share — context for which NbS suits the agrarian structure.
MapSPAM-style gridded production value — context on what's at stake economically.

T7_geographic_context.description: Humid tropical zone with > 1200mm annual rainfall and no prolonged dry season. Dominant farming systems: tree crops, mixed rainfed, root crops. Agroforestry widely practised (cocoa, coffee, home gardens). Primary slope constraint is more relaxed due to high biomass and erosion control provided by tree cover.

Sub-humid tropical zone with 800–1200mm annual rainfall and a defined dry season (3–5 months). Dominant farming systems: maize-mixed, mixed rainfed, agropastoral. Classic zone for agroforestry with leguminous trees (Gliricidia, Leucaena, Faidherbia). Drought is a seasonal constraint requiring careful species selection.

Semi-arid zone with 300–800mm annual rainfall and 5–8 month dry season. Aridity index 0.2–0.5. Dominant systems: agropastoral millet/sorghum, pastoral. Parkland agroforestry (Faidherbia albida, Vitellaria paradoxa) and FMNR are the appropriate sub-types. Drought is the primary constraint; rainfall threshold overrides are required. Haile (2024) and Ahmad et al. (2020) demonstrate viable agroforestry in this zone.

Highland tropical zone at > 1500m elevation with cooler temperatures (mean annual temp 10–18°C) and typically 800–1500mm annual rainfall. Dominant systems: highland mixed, root crops, tea/coffee. Slope constraints are more relaxed for agroforestry due to traditional terracing practices. Nath et al. (2021) and Mushtaq et al. (2023) cover this zone in the Eastern Himalayas and Kashmir respectively.

Temperate European zone with 500–900mm annual rainfall and seasonal temperature variation. Mediterranean and Atlantic climate subtypes. Dominant systems: cereal-based mixed farming, permanent crops, silvopasture. Palma et al. (2007) and Kiziridis et al. (2026) cover silvoarable and silvopastoral systems in this zone. Temperature lower bounds apply for frost-sensitive species.

Sub-humid tropical zone specific to sub-Saharan Africa context where road infrastructure is sparse and market access constraints require more relaxed suitability thresholds. Chuma et al. (2021) DRC and Ahmad & Goparaju (2017) India studies apply here. Road access distance override is wider than global default. Farming systems: mixed rainfed, agropastoral, tree crop.

Hyperarid and arid zones with < 250mm annual rainfall and aridity index < 0.2. WH is among the very few viable agricultural NbS. Dominant sub-practices: wadi-based check dams, cisterns, micro-catchments, Vallerani technique. Evaporation losses are high and must be factored into storage sizing. Primary context for Iraq, Jordan, Saudi Arabia, and Yemen papers in the stocktake (Adham et al. 2018, Hassan et al. 2025, Al-Shabeeb et al. 2022, Al-Shamiri & Ziadat 2012).

T7_geographic_context.context_name: Middle East and North Africa dryland (MENA arid)
T7_geographic_context.description: MENA-specific arid context characterised by wadi systems, limestone geology, and traditional RWH heritage (cisterns, spate irrigation). The largest geographic cluster in the WH stocktake: Iraq (Adham 2018, Hassan 2025, multiple others), Jordan (Al-Shabeeb 2022, Alrawi 2023), Palestine (Shadeed 2019), Yemen (Al-Shamiri 2012), Saudi Arabia. Wadi stream-order suitability applies; geology/lineament density is a key additional input variable used by MENA papers but absent in global default T4.

South Asian semi-arid context covering Deccan Plateau, Rajasthan, and Pakistan dryland zones. Government subsidy schemes (MGNREGS) substantially reduce effective smallholder WH costs. Check dams and percolation tanks are the dominant government-promoted sub-practices. Key papers: Singh et al. (2016), Vema et al. (2019), Jha et al. (2014), Zheng et al. (2023) Pakistan.

T7_geographic_context.context_name: East Africa semi-arid and sub-humid (Ethiopia, Tanzania, Rwanda)
T7_geographic_context.description: East African dryland context spanning Ethiopia, Tanzania, Kenya, and Rwanda. The second-largest geographic cluster in the WH stocktake. Strong government and NGO support for WH through IWMI, CGIAR, and national programmes. Key papers: Alene et al. (2022), Bera equivalent in Ethiopia, Mahmoud et al. (2014), Grum et al. (2016), Mbilinyi et al. (2007), Umugwaneza et al. (2022), Amdihun et al. (2014), Worku et al. (2020).
Sierra Leone national boundary (agroforestry pilot AOI).
EO-derived: rainfed cropland (cropland + no significant irrigation + low tree cover + low livestock density). Default for rainfed staple systems.
EO-derived: cropland + GMIA irrigation share above threshold. Paddy, irrigated horticulture, irrigation schemes.
EO-derived: cropland co-located with moderate-to-high livestock density (GLW). Mixed smallholder systems incl. highland-mixed, maize-mixed.
EO-derived: sparse cropland + grazing-dominant land cover + livestock-dominant economy. Sahelian millet/sorghum + livestock contexts.
EO-derived: grassland/shrubland-dominant with high livestock density and minimal cropland. Pastoralist systems.
EO-derived: tree-perennial-crop dominant (cocoa, coffee, oil palm, rubber, fruit) — Hansen tree cover + MapSPAM perennial mask.

T0_nbs_registry.description_short: Deliberate integration of trees and shrubs into crop and/or livestock farming systems to generate ecological, economic, and social benefits. Encompasses a diverse family of practices from parkland intercropping to silvopastoralism.
T0_nbs_registry.description_technical: Agroforestry encompasses intentional management of woody perennials (trees, shrubs, palms, bamboos) in spatial or temporal combinations with agricultural crops and/or animals on the same land unit. Functional interactions between components are exploited to improve productivity, diversify income streams, sequester carbon, regulate microclimate and hydrology, and enhance biodiversity. Sub-practices include silvoarable (alley cropping, intercropping), silvopastoral (parkland, agrosilvopastoral), and special-purpose systems (windbreaks, riparian buffers, forest farming, home gardens). Suitability is determined by biophysical constraints (climate, soil, slope) and socio-economic context (tenure, market access, labour).

upfront_cost_usd_ha.source_note: Ranges from low-input parkland systems (~$150/ha) to intensively managed silvoarable systems in temperate contexts (~$1200/ha). Marinis et al. (2022) report NPV of CAD$1.186 billion across a landscape-scale intervention. Unger & Lakes (2023) quantify implementation cost savings of assisted natural regeneration vs. tree planting.

T3_nbs_hazard_farming.mitigation_mechanism: Tree canopies reduce evapotranspiration from crop and soil surfaces through shading, lower temperatures under canopy by 2–5°C, and improve soil water retention via increased organic matter and macropore structure from root channels. Hydraulic lift by deep-rooted trees moves water from moist subsoil to drier upper layers, maintaining moisture availability to interplanted crops during dry spells.
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.caveats: Mitigation effect requires canopy closure; limited in first 2–3 years post-establishment. In extreme arid zones (AI <0.2), tree water demand can compete with crops and reduce overall system productivity unless drought-tolerant species are selected.
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.justification: Brandt et al. (2015) identify agroforestry as a top-priority CSA practice for drought-prone mixed rainfed systems in East Africa. Ahmad et al. (2020) demonstrate high land potential for agroforestry in South Asian rainfed systems where drought is the primary climate barrier. Mendonça et al. (2022) map agroforestry as a high-priority drought adaptation practice in Brazilian rain-fed landscapes.

T3_nbs_hazard_farming.mitigation_mechanism: Parkland agroforestry (e.g., Faidherbia albida, Vitellaria paradoxa) is a traditional drought adaptation strategy in Sahelian and sub-Saharan agropastoral systems. Retained trees provide nitrogen fixation, organic matter, shade, and fodder during dry seasons. Farmer-managed natural regeneration (FMNR) of parkland trees has demonstrably increased crop yields under rainfall stress.
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.caveats: Species selection critical — reverse-phenology trees (e.g., Faidherbia) that shed leaves during growing season are most compatible with crops. Requires tenure security for farmers to invest in tree retention.
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.justification: Haile (2024) demonstrates parkland agroforestry as the most suitable NbS for semi-arid agropastoral zones in Ethiopia. Brandt et al. (2015) confirm agroforestry priority in drought-exposed Turkana and Wajir counties (arid agropastoral). Ahmad & Goparaju (2017) show high agroforestry suitability in Jharkhand dryland zones.

T3_nbs_hazard_farming.mitigation_mechanism: Riparian buffer strips and waterway-adjacent agroforestry systems reduce peak runoff by intercepting rainfall, increasing infiltration, and stabilising streambanks. Root systems of perennial trees increase soil macroporosity and infiltration rates. Windbreak and alley cropping systems slow surface water flow across slopes, reducing runoff velocity and peak discharge at watershed scale.
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.caveats: Effect on peak flood discharge is meaningful only at catchment scale with significant landscape coverage (>20–30% of watershed area). Poorly designed systems in floodplains can increase risk during extreme events. Castle et al. (2025) flag floodplain areas as unsuitable for agroforestry siting due to structural damage risk.
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.justification: Baldwin et al. (2022) demonstrate integrated agroforestry and FloodWise practices (dry dams, riparian buffers) for flood mitigation in North Carolina, using spatial optimisation to site systems away from high-hazard floodplains. Castle et al. (2025) include riparian buffers as flood-risk-reducing agroforestry sub-practice. Ahmad et al. (2020) map agroforestry co-benefit for flood adaptation in South Asian peri-urban landscapes.

T3_nbs_hazard_farming.mitigation_mechanism: Tree canopy within or adjacent to paddy systems reduces air and soil surface temperatures through shading and evapotranspiration, creating cooler microclimates. Shade trees reduce heat stress on workers and livestock. Fruit and timber trees in paddy bunds provide income diversification buffering climate-induced crop yield losses.
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.caveats: Dense shading reduces paddy yields; optimal tree density and placement must avoid excessive shading of rice crop. Effect strongest in regions with heat index regularly exceeding 35°C. Literature evidence is primarily from South and Southeast Asia.
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.justification: Ahmad et al. (2018) assess agroforestry suitability in Bihar including irrigated areas. Nath et al. (2021) include irrigated zones in Eastern Himalayan agroforestry suitability mapping where heat stress in lower elevation farming systems is a growing concern under climate change.

T3_nbs_hazard_farming.mitigation_mechanism: Tree canopy provides direct cooling of crop microclimate, reducing soil surface temperatures by up to 10°C and ambient air temperatures by 2–5°C. Shading reduces evaporative stress on annual crops. Shade provision for livestock prevents heat-induced productivity and mortality losses in silvo-pastoral systems.
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.caveats: Species selection must balance shade provision with crop light requirements. Effect limited in early establishment phase.
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.justification: Yadav et al. (2024) explicitly model temperature stress layers (Tmax_May, Tmax_Jan) as key agroforestry suitability and hazard variables in Punjab. Kiziridis et al. (2026) map silvopastoral systems in Spain and Greece partly in response to increasing heat stress on open pasture systems.

T3_nbs_hazard_farming.mitigation_mechanism: Silvopastoral agroforestry systems can reduce fire spread through management of grass fuel loads by grazing and by creating heterogeneous fire breaks. However, woody biomass accumulation in poorly managed systems can increase fire intensity. Fire risk management depends critically on species selection and stocking rates.
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.caveats: Poorly managed agroforestry systems with dense woody understorey and dry grass accumulation can INCREASE fire risk in savanna/dry forest contexts. Yadav et al. (2024) explicitly include wildfire susceptibility as a constraint layer, excluding high-fire-risk areas from agroforestry prioritisation. Context-specific species selection (non-flammable vs. resinous species) is critical.
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.justification: Yadav et al. (2024) model wildfire susceptibility as a suitability constraint, treating high fire risk areas as hazard barriers to agroforestry. Nath et al. (2021) note fire risk as a spatial exclusion criterion in fire-prone parts of the Eastern Himalayas. Kiziridis et al. (2026) consider silvopastoral systems in fire-prone Mediterranean contexts where fire risk is a design constraint.

T3_nbs_hazard_farming.mitigation_mechanism: Deep-rooted trees increase soil water extraction and drainage through root channels and macropore formation. Trees on terraces and bunds in highland systems stabilise slopes and reduce surface waterlogging. Agroforestry systems with well-drained soils on sloped terrain naturally shed excess water more effectively than flat monocultures.
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.caveats: In very poorly drained soils or flatland systems, trees may not provide drainage benefit and some species may not tolerate waterlogged conditions. Soil drainage class is a key suitability determinant (Wotlolan et al. 2021, Haris et al. 2021).
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.justification: Wotlolan et al. (2021) include drainage as a key suitability criterion for agroforestry in Fiji highlands. Mushtaq et al. (2023) include soil drainage class as a suitability variable for mulberry agroforestry in Himalayan Kashmir. Seja et al. (2022) map drainage as a land suitability criterion for agroforestry allocation in Tanzania.

T3_nbs_hazard_farming.mitigation_mechanism: In existing tree crop systems (cocoa, coffee, oil palm), diversification with additional multi-purpose trees creates a layered canopy providing mutual shade, humidity retention, and microclimate buffering. Shade-grown coffee and cacao agroforestry systems show greater resilience to drought years than monocultures through reduced evaporative demand.
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.caveats: Species must be compatible with existing plantation crop. Farmer uptake requires clear economic co-benefits to justify canopy management costs.
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.justification: Singh et al. (2024) identify tree crop systems as a key context for scaling agroforestry using remote sensing to map adoption potential. Palma et al. (2007) demonstrate economic and environmental co-benefits of silvoarable systems in European tree crop contexts.

T3_nbs_hazard_farming.mitigation_mechanism: Windbreaks reduce wind velocity downstream for distances up to 15-20 times the height of the shelterbelt, mitigating wind-induced soil erosion and protecting crops from lodging.
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.caveats: Requires strategic spacing and layout; selection of species that do not compete heavily with interplanted crops.
T3_nbs_hazard_farming.justification: Cleugh (1998) shows that windbreaks shelter crops from mechanical wind stress, reducing wind speeds by 20-50%. Sudmeyer et al. (2004) confirm yield increases in wind-sheltered zones.

Land cover is an eligibility filter, not a graduated suitability variable. Cropland (class 40) and grassland/pasture (20, 30) are the primary targets for agroforestry integration. Built-up, water, ice, and mangroves are hard exclusions. Used as the primary land-use mask in Ahmad et al. (2020), Castle et al. (2025), Baldwin et al. (2022), and the majority of stocktake papers.

Strict nature reserves (IUCN I–II) prohibit agricultural intervention including agroforestry. Hard exclusion required regardless of biophysical suitability. Chuma et al. (2021) use the Itombwe Nature Reserve boundary as a hard exclusion layer in DR Congo agroforestry suitability mapping. Pinto et al. (2019) use protected area boundaries as planning zones in Montado conservation prioritisation.

T6_nbs_scorecard.effect_mechanism: Agroforestry diversifies farm income through timber, fruit, fodder, and non-timber forest products, and can provide direct dietary benefits from edible trees. Income diversification buffers against crop failure events and reduces seasonal food insecurity. Long-term carbon revenue streams (where accessible) can provide additional cash income to smallholders.
T6_nbs_scorecard.conditionality: Poverty reduction effect is conditional on functional market access for agroforestry products and sufficient farm size to absorb tree integration without reducing food crop area below subsistence thresholds. Tenure security required for smallholders to invest in long-rotation tree crops.

economic_value_range.source_note: Wide range reflects variation from subsistence parkland systems (~$50/ha/yr in additional income) to intensively managed silvoarable or carbon-credited systems (~$500/ha/yr). Marinis et al. (2022) report NPV of CAD $1.186 billion at landscape scale. Palma et al. (2007) demonstrate positive iNPV for European silvoarable systems.

T6_nbs_scorecard.justification: Ahmad & Goparaju (2017) position agroforestry explicitly as a livelihood improvement and poverty reduction strategy in Jharkhand, India. Brandt et al. (2015) identify agroforestry as a CSA practice that directly targets food security and income stability in Kenya's poorest counties. [archetype: Long horizon archetype: 5–15 years to positive net cashflow; patient capital, carbon finance, or output-based aid required during establishment phase. CrossBoundary classification: long_horizon.]

T6_nbs_scorecard.effect_mechanism: Trees in agroforestry systems sequester carbon in aboveground biomass (stem, branches, leaves) and belowground (roots). Agroforestry also improves soil organic carbon through litter inputs, root turnover, and reduced tillage frequency. Compared to open cropland, agroforestry systems store 2–5× more carbon per hectare in mature states.
T6_nbs_scorecard.conditionality: Carbon sequestration rates vary widely by species, climate, soil, and management. High rates apply only after canopy closure (typically 5–10 years post-establishment). Permanence requires long-term tree retention and management.

economic_value_range.source_note: Based on sequestration rates of 1–10 t CO₂/ha/yr × carbon price of $10–30/t. Kiziridis et al. (2026) map silvopastoral carbon sequestration potential for climate policy. Mendonça et al. (2023) use SOC sequestration potential as the primary prioritisation criterion.

T6_nbs_scorecard.justification: Kiziridis et al. (2026) explicitly optimise agroforestry siting to maximise carbon sequestration in Spain and Greece. Mendonça et al. (2023) use SOC sequestration as the leading environmental criterion for prioritising agroforestry areas in Brazil. Palma et al. (2007) demonstrate carbon sequestration as a primary environmental performance metric for European silvoarable systems. [archetype: Carbon revenue streams begin only after MRV verification (typically 3–5 years post-establishment). Carbon finance requires upfront technical assistance costs of $50–200/ha. Most viable for large-scale aggregated schemes.]

T6_nbs_scorecard.effect_mechanism: Agroforestry diversifies farm output to include fruits, nuts, fodder, and fuelwood alongside staple crops, reducing household food insecurity. Tree shade moderates heat stress on crops, and improved soil organic matter from leaf litter increases long-term soil fertility and yield stability. In drought years, deep-rooted trees can maintain partial productivity while annual crops fail.
T6_nbs_scorecard.conditionality: Food security benefit requires that tree integration does not compete excessively with food crops for light and water, especially during crop establishment. Compatible species pairing and appropriate tree density are critical. Effect may be negative in the short term (1–3 years) if tree establishment reduces crop area.
T6_nbs_scorecard.justification: Brandt et al. (2015) identify food security improvement as the primary CSA outcome targeted by agroforestry in East Africa. Ahmad et al. (2020) frame the South Asia agroforestry scale-up explicitly around food security and climate resilience co-benefits. [v0.3.0 reroute: food_security_risk → production_gap per T5 ratification (issue #34); see methodology/opportunity_space_T5.md.]

T6_nbs_scorecard.effect_mechanism: Agroforestry introduces structural complexity and functional diversity into agricultural landscapes, creating habitat for invertebrates, birds, and small mammals. Tree species diversity in agroforestry systems supports pollinator communities and pest-control predators. Riparian and corridor agroforestry enables connectivity between fragmented natural habitat patches.
T6_nbs_scorecard.conditionality: Biodiversity benefit requires diverse tree species selection (not monoculture timber plantations). Greatest benefit in landscapes highly degraded by intensive monoculture agriculture with little remaining natural habitat. Effect may be limited near intact natural forests where conversion to agroforestry could reduce habitat quality.
T6_nbs_scorecard.justification: Pinto et al. (2019) demonstrate agroforestry (Montado) as the primary biodiversity conservation vehicle in Portugal's agricultural landscapes, using spatial prioritisation to identify high-value agroforestry zones. Chuma et al. (2021) site agroforestry in the buffer zone of the Itombwe Reserve to support wildlife habitat connectivity. Nath et al. (2021) include biodiversity indicators in the Eastern Himalayan agroforestry suitability framework.

T6_nbs_scorecard.effect_mechanism: Tree root systems bind soil and increase macroporosity, reducing surface runoff and water erosion. Leaf litter and organic matter inputs increase soil organic carbon, improving soil structure, water retention, and fertility. Windbreak and shelterbelt agroforestry systems reduce wind erosion. Nitrogen-fixing trees (Leucaena, Faidherbia, Gliricidia) improve soil nitrogen status, reducing fertiliser dependency.
T6_nbs_scorecard.conditionality: Soil protection effect requires established canopy and functioning root systems (typically 3–7 years post-establishment). Effect may be delayed or limited in very degraded, compacted, or saline soils that require additional soil rehabilitation inputs.

economic_value_range.source_note: Avoided fertiliser costs and soil remediation costs. Castle et al. (2025) use soil erosion reduction (GloSEM model) as a primary agroforestry co-benefit. Palma et al. (2007) include nitrate leaching reduction and erosion control in European silvoarable economic assessment.

T6_nbs_scorecard.justification: Castle et al. (2025) prioritise agroforestry in areas with high water and wind erosion risk as the primary targeting criterion. Palma et al. (2007) demonstrate significant reduction in soil erosion (RUSLE) and nitrate leaching as environmental performance metrics. Mendonça et al. (2022, 2023) include soil organic carbon and slope stability as priority criteria. [archetype: Soil improvement benefits compound over time; economic value realised through reduced input costs and sustained yield maintenance over decades.]

T6_nbs_scorecard.effect_mechanism: Agroforestry tree canopy reduces evapotranspiration from crop and soil surfaces, lowers ambient temperature by 2–5°C under canopy, and hydraulic lift by deep-rooted trees moves subsoil water to upper layers. Combined effects maintain soil moisture during dry spells and reduce crop heat stress. Parkland agroforestry in semi-arid Sahel shows 20–80% higher crop yields under drought conditions compared to open fields.
T6_nbs_scorecard.conditionality: Drought mitigation effect strongest in semi-arid to sub-humid contexts (AI 0.3–0.65). In humid zones, drought is less limiting so the marginal benefit is smaller. Effect requires established trees (3–7 years establishment).
T6_nbs_scorecard.justification: Brandt et al. (2015) demonstrate agroforestry as the top-ranked CSA practice for drought mitigation in East Africa's dryland farming systems. Mendonça et al. (2022) map agroforestry as a high-priority drought adaptation measure in Brazil. Ahmad et al. (2020) quantify land potential for scaling agroforestry in South Asia specifically in drought-exposed rainfed systems.

T6_nbs_scorecard.effect_mechanism: Agroforestry establishment requires upfront investment in planting material (seedlings), labour for planting, establishment maintenance, and often fencing or protection from livestock browsing. Costs are higher for intensively managed silvoarable systems compared to low-input parkland or FMNR systems.
T6_nbs_scorecard.conditionality: Establishment cost is highly variable: FMNR and farmer-managed regeneration systems have near-zero direct costs. High-value commercial silvoarable systems (walnut, oak, fruit trees) have establishment costs of $500–1500/ha. Subsidies, carbon finance, or output-based aid can offset establishment costs.

economic_value_range.source_note: Low end: FMNR systems with minimal planting material cost. High end: intensively managed commercial silvoarable systems in Europe (Palma et al. 2007). Marinis et al. (2022) report detailed NPV calculations including silviculture costs for DR Congo context.

T6_nbs_scorecard.justification: Baldwin et al. (2022) build an explicit habitat creation cost surface to optimise agroforestry siting under budget constraints. Unger & Lakes (2023) quantify implementation cost savings between natural regeneration and active tree planting. Marinis et al. (2022) calculate full NPV including silviculture costs. [archetype: CrossBoundary long_horizon archetype: large upfront establishment costs with returns deferred 5–15 years. Requires patient capital, output-based aid, or advance carbon credit payments to bridge establishment financing gap.]

T6_nbs_scorecard.effect_mechanism: Proximity to road networks and markets enables farmers to access seedlings, technical inputs, and extension services for agroforestry establishment, and to sell timber, fruit, and non-timber forest products at commercially viable prices. Market access is a key determinant of whether agroforestry investment generates returns sufficient to justify the long payback period.
T6_nbs_scorecard.conditionality: Market access effect is strongest for commercial agroforestry sub-types (timber, fruits). Subsistence-oriented parkland agroforestry is less sensitive to road access. Effect is strongly context-dependent: carbon credit access is less dependent on road proximity than timber markets.
T6_nbs_scorecard.justification: Chuma et al. (2021) include road access distance as an explicit suitability criterion, recognising market access as a key system constraint. Castle et al. (2025) include infrastructure connectivity as a social-ecological suitability layer. Brandt et al. (2015) identify market linkages as a key enabling condition for CSA/agroforestry uptake in East Africa. [archetype: Market access is a prerequisite for commercial viability of long-horizon investments. Without market linkages, agroforestry remains subsistence-scale and carbon credit participation is unlikely.]

T0_nbs_registry.description_short: Deliberate capture, storage, and managed use of surface runoff and rainwater through structural and land-based interventions to improve agricultural water availability, recharge groundwater, and reduce erosion and flood risk.
T0_nbs_registry.description_technical: Water harvesting and conservation encompasses interventions that intercept, retain, and store precipitation and surface runoff for productive use or groundwater recharge. Structural sub-practices include check dams, farm ponds, percolation tanks, gully plugs, earthen and small dams, and cisterns. Land-based sub-practices include contour bunding, terracing, tied ridging, contour trenching, and micro-catchment systems. Site selection is determined primarily by slope, drainage morphology, runoff potential (SCS Curve Number), soil texture, and rainfall. The dominant spatial method across 85 stocktake papers is GIS-based multi-criteria evaluation (MCE) using AHP-weighted overlay validated against DEM-derived hydrological parameters.

upfront_cost_usd_ha.source_note: Low end: simple contour bunds at community labour cost (~$50/ha). High end: engineered check dams or lined farm ponds (~$800/ha). Teréncio et al. (2017) calculate EUR/m3 storage cost ratios. Ibrahim et al. (2019) apply cost-minimisation in dam site selection. Ahmed et al. (2023) monetise ecosystem-service benefits in USD.
